findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who'd buy this — add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even informal ones count) where villa developers or building managers confirmed they're frustrated with current solar systems failing, ideally with a quote like "We had to replace our system after two years due to corrosion" or a note that you have 2-3 developers interested in piloting your solution once it's ready.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they're sitting at the bottom like supporting facts rather than the reason this moment is critical—add a timeline showing when these forces converge. For example, add a small section that says "Why Now: UAE Climate Law enforcement begins 2026 + Estidama Phase 3 mandate kicks in 2025 + $2.3B villa pipeline in next 24 months = developers need compliant, reliable systems immediately or face regulatory penalties and project delays." This transforms passive market conditions into active urgency that explains why investors should fund you today, not in two years.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature list—you need to show *how* the AI actually works on a real problem. Add one concrete example: walk through what happens when an installer in, say, Dubai inputs a villa's roof size and hot water demand into HelioHelp, and show the specific output (e.g., "system recommends 8 collectors, predicts 18,000 kWh annual savings, flags hard-water polymer requirement")—this transforms it from "AI-guided sizing" into proof that the engine delivers differentiated answers that a generic tool wouldn't.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You're building a platform that depends on AI to scale globally, but investors won't know if HelioHelp runs on your own infrastructure or relies on third-party APIs like OpenAI, AWS, or Google Cloud — and that matters for unit economics and risk. Add a single line to Slide 19 under "Core Capabilities" or "Strategic Impact" that clarifies this: for example, "Proprietary AI engine deployed on [your infrastructure / AWS / etc.] ensures data privacy and cost control" or "Leverages [specific cloud provider] with custom model fine-tuning to reduce per-deployment inference costs by X%." This removes a major assumption investors will otherwise fill in themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi) with mandated solar installations and commercial deployments handling customer data through your AI platform — add one slide in the appendix that covers your data residency approach, how you handle customer energy data in HelioHelp, and any relevant certifications (ISO 27001, GDPR compliance if serving EU installers, or local UAE data requirements). For example: "HelioHelp Data Security: Customer system data encrypted at rest and in transit | Data residency: UAE servers for GCC deployments | ISO 27001 certification roadmap Q3 2026 | Compliance: ADISA guidelines for Abu Dhabi utilities" —

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-page assumptions sheet that shows the math behind your 2030 projections—specifically: how many units you're selling each year (starting from your current 50-unit target), what price per unit, customer acquisition cost per channel (developers vs. ESCOs vs. direct), and how your gross margin scales from 35% in 2026 to 45% by 2028 (tie this to your injection molding partnership and manufacturing ramp). For example, your Base case could show "2030: 800 units at $8,400/unit via developer partnerships (60% of revenue) + ESCO contracts (40%), with CAC of $1,200 declining

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and the Rixos payback period, but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand if your unit economics actually work at scale—right now it looks like you're showing product margins, not customer profitability. Add a simple table showing: for a typical hotel customer (like Rixos), what's your customer acquisition cost, what's the lifetime value including hardware + O&M contracts, and what's the ratio—for example, "Hotel CAC: $15K | LTV: $180K (hardware + 5-year O&M) | LTV:CAC: 12x" so investors can see you're not just making margin on each unit

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: The $213K contracted on Slide 12 is your locked-in near-term revenue, but your 2026 hardware revenue projection of $0.3M ($300K) needs to explicitly account for this—add a footnote on Slide 14 saying "2026 includes $213K contracted Rixos + EtihadWE deployments (Q2 delivery) plus $87K from pipeline conversion" so investors see how you're bridging from signed deals to your full-year forecast, not a mysterious $87K gap.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated "Use of Funds" slide that breaks down exactly how you'll deploy this new investment across the next 18 months—for example: "Manufacturing setup & inventory: $120K | Sales & operations hires: $80K | Pilot support & installation: $70K | Working capital: $30K" tied directly to hitting your 50-installation target and Q2 2026 deployment milestones shown on Slide 12.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line under each customer name showing either the contract value, installation date, or payment status — for example, "Rixos: $180K contract signed, installation Q2 2024" and "EtihadWE: $120K annual licensing, payments commenced Jan 2024." This transforms them from project names into proof points that money is actually flowing.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now your traction slide shows capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual revenue momentum—add a simple metric like "Revenue grew from $X in Month 1 to $Y in Month 3" or "Rixos project generated $Z in Q1, up from $0 in Q4" to prove the business is accelerating, not just positioned to scale. This one concrete number will make the unit economics you're showing feel real rather than theoretical.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to your Rixos case study: something like "System uptime: 99.2% | Customer satisfaction: 9/10 NPS" or "Zero unplanned maintenance in 12 months of operation." This proves your reliability claim isn't just engineering talk; it's real-world validated, and it gives investors confidence that customers will stick around once you scale.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Name 3-4 actual competitors in the GCC market instead of "Legacy Solar" and "Heat Pumps" — for example, replace those categories with something like "Solahart flat-roof systems, Daikin heat pumps, and Rheem electric storage" so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can validate your claims against real products they might know.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, with competitors shown as weaker (▲ or ●). No explanation is provided for why SolarisKit achieves superior performance across all these disparate technical dimensions.

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table is solid, but it only shows product categories, not the actual companies that could crush you—add a row at the bottom identifying who the real threats are (e.g., "Bosch entering flat-roof solar," "Tesla scaling heat pumps in GCC," "Local HVAC giants pivoting to hybrid systems") and briefly note why your defensibility holds against each one. This transforms your feature comparison into a credible competitive moat story.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors believe you'll execute—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas your core team doesn't cover (like fundraising experience, enterprise sales into utilities, or regulatory/policy expertise in renewable energy). For example, if you're targeting utility partnerships, naming a former utility executive or energy policy advisor would immediately signal you've thought through that channel and have someone in your corner who's done it before.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a simple line to the Team slide stating the vesting schedule for your founders—something like "Faisal and Mark: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff, effective [date]"—so investors know you're aligned for the long haul and won't see this as an oversight. This takes 10 seconds to add and removes a box they'll mentally check while reviewing.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and patents locked down, but investors in regulated markets will want to see explicit compliance pathways for the specific mandates you're betting on—Estidama, Dubai, and Barjeel. Add a slide or section showing which certifications/approvals you already hold for each emirate, which ones you're pursuing by when, and how your design meets (or exceeds) each standard; for example: "Estidama compliance: Design meets water efficiency tier 1 (verified Q1 2026) | Dubai Municipality approval: Application submitted Jan 2026, expected Q2 2026 | Barjeel alignment: Hard-water corrosion testing under

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked in three jurisdictions and a strong IP story, but investors will want to know you've also thought through the legal risks that come with scaling—especially in a regulated market like the UAE with mandates and building codes. Add one slide before the financials that briefly covers your IP protection strategy (trademark status of HelioHelp, any licensing agreements with Rheem or partners, and confirmation that your patents don't infringe on existing solar thermal IP), plus a single sentence on regulatory compliance status for Dubai/Abu Dhabi certifications—this shows you're not just building a great product, you're building it the right way.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the math explicitly so investors see the relationship isn't a separate claim but a logical outcome of your market sizing. Right now it reads like two disconnected figures, which creates doubt about whether your numbers are internally coherent.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, inconsistent slide labeling ('UNKNOWN' on Slide 3)
findings.suggestion: Go back through the entire deck and do a final proofread pass — you've got cut-off text on Slide 6 ("Strong push for dec" should be "Strong push for decarbonisation"), placeholder URLs scattered throughout ([URL_REDACTED] appears on nearly every slide), and Slide 3 is labeled "UNKNOWN" instead of a real title like "SOLUTION" or "TECHNOLOGY." Replace every [URL_REDACTED] with actual image credits or remove them entirely, finish that sentence on Slide 6, and rename Slide 3 to match your content structure — investors notice these details and they undermine your credibility when everything else is this polished.
